network.overall_strategy: This network models adenine as a prebiotic network product rather than a single linear product. It integrates (i) classical HCN oligomerization through DAMN and AICN, (ii) eutectic-freezing concentration of NH4CN/HCN, (iii) formamide-based mineral and photochemical routes that feed the same HCN/AICN chemistry, (iv) Hill-Orgel thermal conversion of DAMN with ammonium formate, and (v) cyanosulfidic nucleoside-first chemistry that yields adenine-containing nucleosides under surface conditions before adenine release by later hydrolytic salvage. Hydrothermal chemistry is used conservatively, consistent with the literature, as a source of reduced C1 feedstocks such as formate and HCN-related precursors rather than as the main adenine accumulation site, because adenine is unstable at high temperature. The central hub is AICN, with HCN, formamide, DAMN, and ammonium formate/formamidine acting as additional hubs linking Hydrothermal, Surface, and Biochemical settings.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent, H2-rich, anoxic; conservative feedstock-generation step rather than adenine-forming step

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-catalyzed reduction of CO2 by H2 on Fe-S surfaces gives formate as a reduced C1 feedstock.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fe-S minerals plausibly reduce CO2 to formate and related C1 products in alkaline vent settings; this supports cross-environment feedstock supply while respecting the instability of adenine at vent temperatures (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Cody et al., 2000; LaRowe & Regnier, 2008; Franiatte et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Surface generation and concentration of HCN from ammonium cyanide

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and montmorillonite as concentrating/photochemical mineral context
conditions.other: eutectic freezing at -78 to -20C or evaporitic concentration; pH 8-9; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: NH4CN provides a concentrated HCN/CN− reservoir; freezing or evaporation increases effective cyanide concentration into the productive polymerization regime.
reactions.reasoning: Adenine synthesis from NH4CN requires concentration control; eutectic freezing and concentrated NH4CN are the experimentally established entry points for productive HCN chemistry (Oró, 1960; Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel, 1967; Levy et al., 2000; Miyakawa, Cleaves & Miller, 2002; Orgel, 2004). Mineral agents here represent plausible surface concentration/adsorption context rather than a claimed stoichiometric catalyst.

reactions.name: HCN dimerization to iminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: base-catalyzed by NH3/CN− in aqueous concentrated cyanide; minerals provide adsorption/concentration surfaces
conditions.other: pH 8-9; concentrated cyanide solution or eutectic brine

reactions.mechanism: CN− attacks HCN to form the HCN dimer iminoacetonitrile under base-catalyzed conditions.
reactions.reasoning: The HCN dimer is the first mechanistically supported intermediate in adenine pentamerization; modern computation validates this step and NH3 lowers the barrier substantially (Kikuchi et al., 2000; Sandström & Rahm, 2023; Roy, Najafian & Schleyer, 2007).

reactions.name: HCN trimerization to aminomalononitrile

conditions.catalyst: base-catalyzed cyanide chemistry on mineral-concentrating surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Sequential cyanide addition converts the HCN dimer to the reactive trimer aminomalononitrile.
reactions.reasoning: AMN is the accepted trimeric intermediate in the Oró/Sanchez-Orgel sequence and is part of the validated computational and experimental HCN network (Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel, 1967; Zubay & Mui, 2001; Sandström & Rahm, 2025/2026).

reactions.name: HCN tetramerization to DAMN

conditions.catalyst: base-catalyzed oligomerization in concentrated cyanide medium with mineral concentration surfaces
conditions.other: pH 8-9; DAMN can accumulate as a stable isolable HCN tetramer

reactions.mechanism: Fourth HCN addition yields the isolable tetramer DAMN.
reactions.reasoning: DAMN is the first stable and experimentally isolable major intermediate in HCN pentamerization and the key precursor for photochemical access to AICN (Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel, 1967; Zubay & Mui, 2001; Sandström & Rahm, 2025/2026).

reactions.name: UV photoisomerization of DAMN to trans-DAMN

conditions.catalyst: UV photochemistry; minerals provide irradiated surface context

reactions.mechanism: DAMN undergoes UV-driven cis/trans photoisomerization as the first stage of conversion toward AICN.
reactions.reasoning: Ferris and Orgel established the UV dependence of the DAMN photochemical sequence, later quantified under young-Sun flux by Michaelian and Simeonov (Ferris & Orgel, 1966; Michaelian & Simeonov, 2022).

reactions.name: Photochemical conversion of trans-DAMN to AICN

conditions.other: three-photon sequence via ketenimine intermediate; young-Sun UV

reactions.mechanism: trans-DAMN proceeds through ketenimine-like excited-state chemistry to give AICN, the central purine hub.
reactions.reasoning: AICN formation from photoexcited DAMN/DAFN is a central validated step in classical adenine synthesis, with significant AICN production under plausible early UV flux (Ferris & Orgel, 1966; Michaelian & Simeonov, 2022).

reactions.name: AICN ring closure with HCN to adenine

conditions.catalyst: NH3 catalysis in bulk water; minerals provide concentration surface

reactions.mechanism: A fifth HCN equivalent contributes the final carbon/nitrogen unit and closes the purine pyrimidine ring system.
reactions.reasoning: This is the canonical final step of adenine pentamerization and the major kinetic bottleneck; NH3 lowers the barrier markedly relative to the uncatalyzed pathway (Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel, 1968; Roy, Najafian & Schleyer, 2007; Armas-Vázquez et al., 2023).

reactions.name: AICN ring closure with formamidine to adenine

conditions.other: formamidine acts as the one-carbon/one-nitrogen donor for ring closure

reactions.mechanism: Formamidine reacts with AICN to close the six-membered ring more effectively than free HCN in many mixtures.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris and Orgel identified formamidine as a productive donor for AICN conversion to adenine, and later literature emphasized its presence in HCN oligomerization mixtures (Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel, 1968; Zubay & Mui, 2001).

reactions.name: Thermal conversion of ammonium formate to formamidine

conditions.other: hot evaporitic or geothermal dry phase

reactions.mechanism: Heating ammonium formate generates formamidine in situ, providing the donor needed for adenine-forming ring closure.
reactions.reasoning: Hill and Orgel interpreted ammonium formate as an in situ precursor to formamidine in their high-yield adenine-forming system (Hill & Orgel, 2002).

reactions.name: Hill-Orgel direct thermal adenine synthesis from DAMN and ammonium formate
reactions.mechanism: DAMN undergoes thermal rearrangement/cyclization with ammonium formate-derived formamidine to produce adenine, bypassing the explicit UV AICN route.
reactions.reasoning: This is the highest-yield prebiotically plausible one-pot adenine synthesis reported from HCN-derived chemistry, giving about 18% adenine at 110C (Hill & Orgel, 2002).

reactions.name: Thermal mineral-catalyzed conversion of formamide to HCN/formamidine feedstock

conditions.other: neat formamide or highly concentrated formamide; 100-160C

reactions.mechanism: Formamide thermally decomposes and self-catalyzes into HCN-like and amidine-bearing intermediates that feed adenine-forming chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide is experimentally established as a one-pot adenine feedstock and mechanistically connects to HCN chemistry through HCONH2 ⇌ HCN + H2O and related amidine-forming routes (Saladino et al., 2001; Saladino et al., 2003; Saladino et al., 2004; Yamada et al., 1978; Slavova et al., 2020; Šponer et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Mineral-catalyzed direct adenine formation from formamide

conditions.other: neat formamide, 100-160C, low but detectable adenine yields

reactions.mechanism: Surface-catalyzed condensation of formamide generates HCN/NH3/HNCO-derived networks that furnish adenine in one-pot chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Adenine formation from neat formamide is robust across multiple mineral catalysts, with TiO2 and montmorillonite among the best documented surface catalysts (Saladino et al., 2001; Saladino et al., 2003; Saladino et al., 2004).

reactions.name: Pyrite-catalyzed hydrothermal-interfacial adenine formation from formamide

conditions.other: formamide-rich mineral interface; 160C; interfacial rather than bulk vent-core setting

reactions.mechanism: Fe-S mineral surfaces catalyze condensation of formamide toward purine products including adenine at elevated temperature.
reactions.reasoning: Saladino and co-workers reported purine formation from formamide on iron-sulfur minerals at 160C; this provides the most literature-grounded hydrothermal-side connection to adenine chemistry, though accumulation remains limited by nucleobase instability at high temperature (Saladino et al., 2008; Levy & Miller, 1998; Franiatte et al., 2008).

reactions.name: UV conversion of AICN to AICA by hydrolysis branch

conditions.other: competing branch from the AICN hub

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis converts AICN to the carboxamide AICA, diverting flux toward other purines.
reactions.reasoning: AICN hydrolysis to AICA is a well-established branch point in the purine network and explains competition between adenine and hypoxanthine-forming chemistry (Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel, 1968; Zubay & Mui, 2001; Barks et al., 2010).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoreduction route from 8-mercaptoadenine to adenine-containing nucleosides

conditions.catalyst: H2S and Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox with MgCl2 in phosphate-buffered anoxic solution

reactions.mechanism: Under cyanosulfidic photoredox conditions, 8-mercaptoadenine-bearing intermediates are photoreduced to adenine nucleosides rather than free adenine.
reactions.reasoning: Xu and co-workers showed highly selective prebiotic formation of deoxyadenosine and related purine nucleosides through 8-mercaptoadenine under H2S/UV conditions, extending to mixed RNA/DNA purine nucleosides (Xu et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Biochemical salvage release of adenine from adenosine

conditions.other: slow hydrolytic or thermal cleavage in concentrated prebiotic pools or interfaces; included as a necessary connecting salvage step

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic cleavage of the N-glycosidic bond releases free adenine from adenosine.
reactions.reasoning: This step is not the productive centerpiece of the literature but is included conservatively to connect nucleoside-first cyanosulfidic chemistry to the adenine target; the literature emphasizes nucleoside formation rather than adenine release, so this node is explicitly a connecting salvage step rather than a primary synthesis route (Xu et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021; Becker et al., 2016; Becker et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Biochemical salvage release of adenine from deoxyadenosine

conditions.other: slow hydrolytic or thermal cleavage in prebiotic pools

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic cleavage of the glycosidic bond releases adenine from deoxyadenosine.
reactions.reasoning: As with adenosine salvage, this is included only to connect experimentally validated nucleoside-first chemistry to the adenine network; it is a plausible degradation/salvage link rather than the main synthetic outcome emphasized by the source literature (Xu et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021).

pathways.name: Classical Oró pentamerization via HCN
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN/NH4CN in aqueous surface settings proceeds through dimer, trimer, tetramer, photochemical AICN formation, and final HCN ring closure to adenine.

pathways.name: AICN plus formamidine closure pathway
pathways.description: Classical HCN oligomerization generates AICN, but final cyclization uses formamidine rather than free HCN.

pathways.name: Hill-Orgel DAMN ammonium formate route
pathways.description: DAMN formed from HCN chemistry reacts directly with ammonium formate under dry heat to give adenine at relatively high yield.

pathways.name: Formamide-to-HCN-to-adenine route
pathways.description: Formamide thermally generates HCN/feedstock that enters the classical oligomerization pathway to adenine.

pathways.name: Formamide-to-formamidine-to-AICN closure route
pathways.description: Formamide supplies formamidine/HCN feedstock, with final ring closure from AICN using formamidine.

pathways.name: Direct mineral-catalyzed formamide adenine synthesis on TiO2
pathways.description: Neat formamide heated on TiO2-rich surface minerals yields adenine directly in one-pot condensation chemistry.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-interfacial Fe-S formamide route
pathways.description: Formamide reacts on pyrite/Fe-S mineral interfaces at elevated temperature to give adenine, providing a hydrothermal-side entry point.

pathways.name: Eutectic-freezing enhanced HCN route
pathways.description: Dilute NH4CN is concentrated by freezing, then follows the classical HCN→DAMN→AICN→adenine pathway.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic deoxyadenosine-first salvage route
pathways.description: Surface cyanosulfidic chemistry forms deoxyadenosine via 8-mercaptoadenine; later biochemical hydrolysis releases adenine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic adenosine-first salvage route
pathways.description: Surface cyanosulfidic chemistry forms adenosine-containing purine nucleosides; later biochemical hydrolysis releases adenine.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest classical adenine pathway in the network and matches the literature well: NH4CN/HCN concentration, HCN oligomerization through DAMN, UV conversion to AICN, and final ring closure to adenine. The central role of AICN is highly literature-consistent. Minor caution: the final AICN + HCN closure is known to be the kinetic bottleneck and low-yielding without strong NH3 assistance, so confidence is high but not perfect.
suggestions: Explicitly encode eutectic freezing or high-concentration conditions for NH4CN/HCN in the pathway conditions.
Include NH3/base catalysis details for the AICN to adenine closure to reflect the known barrier reduction.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is well aligned with the literature emphasis that AICN plus formamidine is a canonical adenine-forming branch. The use of ammonium formate as a precursor to formamidine is plausible and supported by Hill-Orgel logic. The main weakness is that ammonium-formate-to-formamidine is represented somewhat abstractly and may require more explicit thermal/dehydrating conditions to be fully convincing.
suggestions: Specify temperature near the Hill & Orgel regime (~110C) and clarify in situ formamidine generation.
Indicate whether dry-state or concentrated conditions are needed for efficient closure.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the best-supported high-yield adenine pathway in the network. DAMN plus ammonium formate at elevated temperature is explicitly highlighted in the literature as one of the strongest prebiotically plausible adenine-producing reactions, with substantially higher yield than classical Oró chemistry. It is coherent, concise, and chemically well grounded.
suggestions: State the thermal condition explicitly as around 110C and note this route bypasses the photochemical DAMN to AICN step.
Clarify whether the network assumes DAMN is isolated or formed in situ from HCN oligomerization.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route captures the literature-supported idea that formamide can feed into HCN-like chemistry, but it is more indirect and somewhat compressed. The network appropriately uses formamide as a feedstock to HCN/formamidine, yet the exact branching from neat formamide to free HCN under surface conditions is system-dependent and not always the dominant experimentally observed route. Still, overall it is defensible.
suggestions: Differentiate thermal decomposition from mineral-assisted formamide chemistry more clearly.
Add catalyst-specific conditions for formamide decomposition versus condensation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is conceptually strong because it unifies formamide, formamidine, AICN, and adenine as described in the literature. Its weakness is mostly implementation detail: the conversion sequence from formamide to formamidine and onward to adenine is chemically plausible but represented generically rather than with a specific experimentally validated protocol.
suggestions: Anchor the pathway to a specific mineral/catalyst system and temperature window from the formamide literature.
Separate the formamide-to-HCN and formamide-to-formamidine branches if both are intended.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Direct adenine formation from formamide on mineral catalysts, especially TiO2 and related minerals, is strongly literature-supported. As a one-step pathway it is chemically credible, but it is less network-rich and less integrated than the HCN/AICN-centered routes. It should specify the actual catalyst and temperature because outcomes are catalyst-sensitive.
suggestions: Ensure TiO2 or another validated catalyst is explicitly listed for this reaction.
Add the 100-160C temperature regime or UV assistance, depending on the intended literature match.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the weakest major pathway. Iron-sulfur minerals do catalyze formamide condensation to purines, but the literature support is stronger for pyrite/pyrrhotite at around 160C in interfacial or mineral-rich settings than for a robust hydrothermal adenine accumulation pathway. Given adenine instability at elevated hydrothermal temperatures, using Hydrothermal as a direct adenine production environment is only weakly justified and should be treated cautiously.
suggestions: Recast this as a hydrothermal-interfacial feedstock generation route rather than a major adenine accumulation pathway.
If retained, constrain the environment to low-temperature vent peripheries or mineral pores rather than generic hydrothermal settings.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is an excellent literature-aligned variant of the classical HCN route because eutectic freezing is one of the most plausible concentration mechanisms for adenine-forming HCN chemistry. It addresses the concentration and hydrolysis problems better than warm aqueous NH4CN alone, and it remains fully consistent with DAMN/AICN chemistry.
suggestions: Explicitly include frozen/eutectic conditions in the pathway metadata.
Note long timescales and low but geochemically plausible yields.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The nucleoside-first cyanosulfidic logic is strongly literature-supported, especially through 8-mercaptoadenine and photoreduction chemistry. However, the final 'salvage' release of free adenine from deoxyadenosine under prebiotic biochemical conditions is not well supported and appears biologically framed rather than prebiotically validated. As a route to adenine specifically, this pathway is therefore weak.
suggestions: Treat deoxyadenosine as an alternative target/product rather than forcing adenine release.
If adenine release is required, provide a non-enzymatic hydrolytic or mineral-assisted depurination rationale.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: As with P9, the nucleoside-forming step is well aligned with cyanosulfidic literature, but the conversion of adenosine to free adenine is not a well-established prebiotic route in the provided literature. This pathway is more compelling as evidence for adenine-containing nucleosides than for free adenine synthesis itself.
suggestions: Retain adenosine as an endpoint or explicitly justify prebiotic depurination conditions.
Distinguish nucleoside-first synthesis from adenine-base accumulation in pathway framing.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is strong overall and captures the literature insight that adenine is best represented as a convergent network product rather than a single linear synthesis. Topologically it is well connected, with appropriate hubs in HCN, DAMN, AICN, formamidine, and formamide, and it includes multiple convergent routes to adenine. Literature alignment is generally very good for the classical HCN oligomerization, eutectic-freezing concentration, formamide chemistry, AICN centrality, and the high-yield Hill-Orgel route. The main weaknesses are: (1) several reactions are specified too generically relative to the cited literature, especially formamide decomposition and ammonium-formate-to-formamidine conversion; (2) direct hydrothermal adenine formation is overstated given adenine instability in hot vent systems; and (3) the two nucleoside-salvage pathways rely on prebiotic 'biochemical salvage' steps that are not well supported experimentally. Another notable issue is representational: the network summary does not show explicit mineral agents and per-reaction literature citations, both of which were required and matter for full environmental and literature scoring. Because of those omissions and the questionable salvage/hydrothermal adenine endpoints, the network is not quite at approval threshold despite being scientifically sophisticated and largely coherent.
